Anybody here run an info business? Almost all organic. Like, like. Basically, no ads, just all. Organic presence. Yeah, Ryan Doon. Okay, so that was me for a while. So I... Starry copy MBA. I thought that the million dollar a month, except my trophy speech, was a little bit like, seldom granted, it's already going to turn each other off a little bit. Um so I'll just I'll tell the story real quick and then let Sanjay talk about the list. We robbed the fucking bank. For like a year and a half. All organic. From if anyone was here in the Q3 Mastermind last year I talked about how I did all organic webbies. We we had a funnel where it was literally just a tick-tock a day. We would run everyone to a webinar where I would pitch it like $5. 97 or $6.97. and then send a handful of those people up to a 4K offer. We, our first full month doing it, we did 355. Our best month was pretty close to 700. And we were cruising. And I joined Jeremy's inner circle, but also... I was at 20. I was at 20. I got Ryan syndrome. Mike's going down. I was a 24-year-old kid making... like 400 grand a month profit and I was like, 'Why would I need to grow?' What is the point? Why would I do? There's no point running ads. There's no point trying to do anything bigger. I'm chilling. I'm flying private. I have like 500 random watches. I had a cool car. I was chilling. And then. Um, Got a little bit. A little bit lazy with organic, end of 2024. And... just started not working anymore. It was like he was pulling teeth trying to get a sale. So we went from like cruising at like five or six hundred grand a month. to like three ish, 100k a month, and then by beginning of 2025, we were like, 'Okay, some thing's not working. I'm gonna like run out of cash It was very humbling. It wasn't fun. We were losing sales. Guys. And so I actually went out to lunch with Uncle Jay because I think he sensed something was wrong inside of the business. And he was like, 'Hey, listen. Here's what you gotta do. You gotta change up the offer.' You gotta put your dick on the line and start running some ass. And you have to treat this like a real business and not just like a thing that you sell from Instagram stories, because that's not that's not what a business is. That's not what an in a good information business should look like. So we did. We switched it up. We pulled ourselves from the trenches. We went from one 198 in February to 350 something in March to 650 in April and then again in May. And then June was 750. July was 850. And then August was just under 1. 1. And we did that. Thank you. We did that spending, it was like between 10 and 14 a day on call funnel. Something like that. And then we would spend, we would throw down, like, between 15 and 25 on bi-weekly webbies. And um, Couple of lessons real quick about like going from pure organic to pure paid, because that's why I had you guys raise your hands— I think a lot of people. Oh my god. Right now I have a business where I only make money when I post on TikTok or Instagram. The way to switch over. And this is what we learned. We had to create an offer that made sense for people who didn't know who Cardinal Mason was. And it actually wasn't even really that hard because we had the copywriting offer. We just added AI to it. We already had the social proof. We had a bunch of people who were making money from it. We had students who were ripping like $80,000 a month from our biz off, which is crazy. Like, we had some people who were in like 40, 50, 60K a month. Most people between like a five and 15 range, but like, we had a proven system where people could make money. And, um... It's going a little bit more aggressive with income claims on ads. Um, I mean, we're gonna hear more from Sanjay about like what actually worked. Okay, I just had to point that out to Mason, but I'll get into the lessons and takeaways from 5Xing our info business in six months and hitting a million dollars a month. And one just like disclaimer I want to give is that by no means was this like something where we just turned ads on and saw success from day one. There was probably 90-ish days where we were like right above break-even and we were kind of like in this weird limbo. And that's with Mason already having a good or... organic audience. So the entire like preface I just want to give is that like these are lessons that we see now on the other side to help you ideally when you start transitioning into paid and scaling make that iteration process quicker. It's a down buddy. I love you. Oh, okay. Okay, so the prerequisite to all of this is just committing to going big. Obviously, Jeremy talks about this all the time, but the way that I think about it is, like, we all have the same 24 hours in the day. Day and like— when I talk to Jeremy, I have no idea how he likes, possibly, manages what he's doing every day, because he probably has like twice as many clients as me, twice as big as me, runs this entire group, has a fiancé, has a bunch of dogs, and that's like just one thing that always kind of just gets me to think in terms of like higher leverage and just that, even though all this might sound overwhelming on paper, it's really not that hard. Second thing to mention is that going bigger and shooting for like million-dollar months doesn't even really require more work or more effort. It's just playing a different game. So, like, I'd argue that. You're working probably a bit more, but not loads more than... No, the credit cards are working harder, but yeah, my workload is in it's just different inputs. So it's like, instead of just putting all of your eggs in the organic basket and just hoping and praying that everything goes right, way you buy back a lot of control and obviously, like the stakes are a little higher because you have money coming on the card every day. But um, it's it's not necessarily harder; it's just different inputs. Then, like, everyone can just raise your hands—like, do you guys think that your business is one of the biggest parts of your whole life in terms of like daily attention, mental bandwidth, et cetera. So yeah, it's like basically all of us. And the thing that just never really sat right with me is like, I don't want to put 10 hours a day of working hours and probably like 16. Hours a day of like mental bandwidth into something that I know I'm half-assing so that's just kind of like the prerequisite because I know that for a lot of people, whether you're stuck at 200k or 500k or whatever, it might seem daunting to hit that next level, but it's like, why not? If you're already putting all this time and effort into it, you might as well just like squeeze it for all it's got. Um, okay. So basically, Jeremy talks about this a lot, how paid ads are just sophisticated gambling. It's obviously, there's risk involved, et cetera. And at the end of the day, if you want to take gambling for what it is, gamblers end up in one of two positions. Either they end up homeless or they end up making buku bucks. The point I'm trying to make is that there is risk involved with this and with that, being said, you can't just like half-ass it and expect to get good results, like gamblers know. Like the gambler walks up to a blackjack table and throws fifty dollars a hand until he loses five hundred dollars— that's how casinos stay in business. The only people who actually win in gambling are the people who really commit, actually like see it through and have the proper strategy. Honestly, even if you're shooting sheep out of a cannon, like there is a strategy to it. Um, So I don't know why it's going two slides at a time. So basically, my point being is: if you were like a complete square— like 'square' is like a gambling term, but essentially, if you were just like the average person. Who wasn't in this group never did anything that Jeremy talks about or anything that anyone in this group talks about. Like your numbers wouldn't be pretty, and this is why the majority of people don't have success with paid ads. It's because, like, it's not the easiest thing in the world to get to work anymore. So you have to understand that, like, this is where the average person falls. Obviously, you could argue that these numbers would be a little different, but either way, like, the average person who just tries to crack cold traffic on their own, like, slowly but surely, they end up losing money, and then they just become homeless at some point. I guess. Yeah, it's going two slides at a time, whatever. Okay, um, so essentially, the way that I frame all of these like little inputs is that, like, from what we've done over the past six months, there was never like one silver bullet. Oh, we changed this one thing and immediately we just went up into the right. It's all just like marginal gains. Like, it was just like 50 good decisions, 10 bad decisions, the good decision outweighed the bad ones, and you just make marginal gains. So the way that I see it is like you understand that you're gambling, and the way to kind of rig the deck in your favor is to find these successful actions, and just compound them on top of each other. And after enough times of adding up three percent, seven percent, four percent, you get to the point where your business is in a much different place. Um, so this is like your edge on the square. It's like all the people who just try to like do ads put five thousand dollars up like five creatives up and then quit and say it doesn't work. Like, it's because they're not looking for these marginal gains. Um, so then slowly but surely, you take all of your marginal gains, and you break past homelessness, and you get to the point where you Reach where you're making buku bucks. So, okay, so the point being is it's not going to be easy. Otherwise, everyone would be capable of doing it as cliche as it sounds. Next thing, like I said, marginal inputs and improvements compound to go a long way. And the biggest thing that I found is having like macro patients. So it's like understanding that this probably isn't going to work. Day one, month one, even like quarter one, but micro aggressiveness in terms of, like, being relentless in making the changes that need to be made, kind of like just stacking odds in your favor, etc is how you get there. And, like I said, there's a reason why most people can't scale with paid traffic, especially without Jeremy, like you have all the advantages of being in this group. Imagine, if you were just trying to do it on your own. So, over a six-month span, we pivoted the entire offer from freelance copywriting to AI copywriting. We went from majority organic traffic to majority paid. We made drastic changes on the sales team. We tested through a handful of different funnels and conversion mechanisms. We tested through hundreds, if not like almost a thousand ads at this point. Six months and five times the business, so the point I'm trying to make is it wasn't like a cakewalk. Like it definitely uh wasn't like Mason had been sitting on some massive audience— we just liquidated it all. Ones like we we went through the trenches. Um, so if I were to boil it down to five pillars, at a high level, like if you mess up any one of these five things, you probably don't have a good chance of reaching million-dollar months. So the first thing is just traffic: can you generate enough high-intent traffic at a low enough cost? Pretty self-explanatory. Next thing is your offer and niche: attractive enough and ideally riding a tailwind. So I'll talk about what I mean by riding a tailwind, but obviously making that pivot from freelance copy. Which was something that Mason had been selling for like two or three years to AI copywriting. Now, it intrigues a bunch of people in terms of they understand what's going to happen with AI. They're scared of AI. Here's how you can use it to make income. We're sorry. I should add there. A lot of people think that's kind of like a cheap sort of like beta switch where it's like, oh, you just switch the offer. Like, you're just like, you put AI in front of it. We... It's not a completely different offer. It's literally just an easier and better way to get the same result. So it's not like we're just like 'oh, you put a on' and now it's sexy. No, it actually makes more sense as a business. Like, it makes way more sense to run it. Yeah, exactly. Um, so then, beyond the offer, you have your infrastructure. So, like, if you're running a call funnel, I would consider the infrastructure everything from the landing page all the way to the closer being on the call. So, is everything within that funnel optimized, financially modeled, and built for scale? So, financial modeling is something that, for example, we know because we have, like, a $5,000 offer. We don't have the benefit of having like a 20k PIF, so we have a lot more marginal game to pace and play. Therefore, we really have to like have everything airtight to make it work in terms of like maintaining healthy margins. The next thing is sales team. So, can your sales team consistently meet, if not exceed, KPIs and then fulfillment? Are you able to not only fulfill but also tack on LTV after someone purchases once? For example, if your fulfillment's falling apart or if any one of these things are falling apart, respectively, you probably don't have a good chance of getting $2 million a month. So, principle number one is just being input focused. Obviously, sitting here like telling you, 'Oh, this is how you achieve X goal you're all thinking in terms of outcomes. But the way that you get there is only through inputs. So I would say that, like, Business and especially when it's through ads, can be volatile. Obviously, my card isn't the one getting the ad spend, so it's a lot easier for for me to be level-headed. But basically, whether we're doing 70K a day or 7K a day, I'm always just focused on what's the next step to move the ball down the field. and not getting like too high when things are going good or too low when things are going bad, um, and then also just like focusing on the bigger picture, like understanding, like, yes, we could be having a really good day or a really bad day, but like, are we trending in the right direction, week over week, month over month, um. Fuck. Okay, so speed, number or principle, number two, is why speed actually matters. So everyone always talks about like, oh, you have to do things with speed. You have to do things with speed. And me being like. A bit more of a I wouldn't say like a perfectionist but just like a cautious kind of person I hear that as like recklessness, but the reason why speed actually matters in business is because, for you to get from point A, wherever you're at, right now, 200k a month, 500k a month, whatever to a million a month, you have to hit every single level, every single letter in the alphabet, in between. So essentially, all of those different letters are just little iteration points where you have to change this thing, optimize this thing, launch this thing, etc. The point I'm trying to make is is that if you take forever to like go and launch that new funnel, go and launch the new VSL, go and put up the new creatives, all you're doing is just taking what could take someone who's moving really fast. Months take 18 months and you're losing all that time and all that opportunity cost, so this is why being input focused matters. Because as you can imagine, scaling to a million a month doesn't look like this. It looks a lot more like this. So right around here is when you start to book in the one-on-one call with Jeremy. Like, right around there. Okay, so... It's still a joke. Okay. Oh my god. Yeah, this clicker is kind of dweening, bro. Okay, so I actually, I accidentally skipped a slide, but basically this is the thing about iteration cycles. So essentially, you can see this visually, but it's like. If you can get through this entire iteration cycle in six months instead of 18, there's always going to be more problems after this. So it's like the quicker you move through the problems that are on the table right now, the quicker you get to the next problems and you get to scale further. So, principle number three is writing tailwinds versus headwinds. So what I would say is, like, when we had the freelance copywriting offer, it wasn't necessarily a bad offer, but it wasn't taking advantage of something that's happening in the macro setting, whereas, like, if we didn't make that one change, probably none of this would have been possible, because it was just becoming more and more of an uphill battle. And obviously, there was a lot more that went into it than just pivoting the offer. But if we didn't pivot the offer, probably nothing that came after it would have mattered. So principle number four is just like what Jeremy talks about, like tweaker mode. So I want to give Mason a lot of credit just because obviously I have a lot of agency clients so my head is in a bunch of different places at the same time. But for us, we found that the best dynamic is like where the offer owner is hyper aware of what's going on within the business. So, like, to the point where it's like, 'Hey, we're having a bunch of no shows today.' Like, I've worked with a lot of people where, like, they completely check out. Like cost per call is like soaring, lead quality is going bad. And obviously, I'm on top of it, but I shouldn't have to care about it more than the business owner, if that makes sense. Or I shouldn't be the first one to spot it instead. Of the business owner. So being hyper aware of everything that's happening and just really being on top of the pulse of the business on a daily basis. And quick to spot when trajectory is changing— that's how you really like get to the point where you're coming from a proactive place instead of reactive. I'll say too, we make a really fun partnership because I'm frantic. Like, I will actually freak out when we have a couple bad days. I think there was one time we went, like, four hours without welcoming a call from the call funnel. And I was like, 'Dude, we're spending a fucking 10 grand a day.' Like, what's going on? And you're really good at looking over, like, multiple weeks. And I feel like... You need to have like... it's heart rate 150 and heart rate 35 like kind of balance each other out that's like a good way where it's like you have a balance of like okay we need to do this these things quickly but also not overreact when we see like a day's worth of data instead of a week. Yeah. And like, this is a misconception I had when I started, you know, getting more into the paid ad space and like Jeremy and a lot of the best like media buyers and marketers. I've met, like, they have a relatively passive approach with what's going on in the account. Like, we're not sitting and like tweaking stuff every two hours. Like, honestly, it gets to the point where. You're like it's just death by a thousand paper cuts, so we're more focused on like week to week growth, month to month growth, and then obviously, like, days are going to fall where they fall. Um, so the next thing is outlier campaigns. So this kind of goes back to the entire like iteration cycle thing, but essentially, we've found that like eight or nine out of ten campaigns that we launch perform marginally the same, and then like one or two out of ten crush— like Jeremy calls them majority hooks. So essentially, like, we just want to get out 10 batches of ads every month or every six weeks instead of every two or three months, because it speeds up that cycle of us finding the outlier campaigns. So that way, we're not testing a batch every two weeks and we're putting all of our eggs in less baskets. So when it comes to just this overall principle, what I would say is that like eight or nine out of 10 campaigns are going to perform marginally the same. So it's in your best interest to kind of dial in all of the infrastructure, whether that's sales team or just... everything within the funnel, to make sure that the eight or nine campaigns are as profitable as possible. And then also, like I just said, testing ads as frequently as humanly possible. So you find the outlier campaigns monthly instead of quarterly, for example. Principle number six is team culture. So, like our team, like me and Mason, are the oldest two on the whole team, right? Oh, we got some like weird 32-year-old dialers for some reason, but they're hilarious. So, like we have a, I guess, was it 50 people on the sales team or something like that? Um, they all bully each other, and all the dialers will like sit in like a Discord chat until like two in the morning. And they're hitting on like Australian English or New Zealand or whoever the whoever's coming in, like they're dialing from like like all day. It's like 14– 15 hour days, they love it, and they love each other. It's really fun. Yeah, so what I would say is that, like, obviously not all of you guys have teams filled with 18 and 20 year olds that are all like screaming and slack all day. But at the end of the day, like, culture is culture, and I would say the biggest levers are the biggest things to kind of optimize for, like tight communication loops, just because everyone on our team is very comfortable with one another. So it's like, we're never like hesitant to like bring something up if we feel like something's going wrong or it just keeps communication really tight. And then the second thing is obviously just having like a competitive spirit. Like, most of these kids need the money, so like, they're you know, they're hungry, but they're also competitive. So principle number seven is this is probably like the like. White rabbit of everything. Is just like messaging bro. Yes, okay. Wait real quick. And by the way, this isn't much longer. I know you guys are getting antsy. Everyone says, Jerry says specifically, that when you switch to paid, everyone's going to get a whole lot colder and that you're going to wish that you had more organic because paid is hard. Paid is... terrible, grueling. We realized that with the right messaging on ads, we were getting warmer and better. richer prospects onto the calendar than we were with a fucking Instagram story. Because exactly what you say in the ad is pretty much exactly what you'll get on your calendar. So you can literally just say some magic spells. Like it's honestly like magic. You could say the right words in the right order. For example, back in November, last November, we were still trying to figure out, 'Hey, we had no idea what we were doing.' I had an ad that mentioned JK Rowling. And it was an ad promoting a copywriting program. And we would get a bunch of people on the calendar that wanted to be authors. Just because we said JK Rowling, we were like, 'Wow, I didn't realize how sensitive Facebook was.' So it's crazy what good messaging can do and how fast it can switch everything for you. It's crazy. Yeah, so I would say, for like, who in here targets like B2C just like mass market offers, like you're selling to people who make ballpark 100k a year, give or take. So, what I would say is like, it's a very fine line, and this is probably one of the biggest like Achilles heels that's not infrastructure related. So, like, obviously, there's certain metrics that like, oh, like show rate, close rate, cost per call, whatever. But what I would say is like, one of the more intangible things that people and, like, we've had trouble in the past really nailing down is the messaging. Um, but what I would say is that, like, our messaging in January versus now is night and day, and, like, as someone who has kind of gone through all the iterations, it's definitely a big reason why most people mess up with paid is because they can't figure this out. Um, The next thing, and we kind of already talked about this, but it's just like creative velocity is like probably one of the biggest things that we just focus on. Like on our account, we have usually no less than 100 direct response ads running at a time. So it's kind of the same thing I talked about with the outlier principles. It's like the reason why we have 100 different ads running at the same time. Same time is obviously because of Andromeda, but also just because we know that if we take a hundred shots at a dartboard, like we're gonna hit two or three, as opposed to if we're only taking 20 shots. So it's definitely in your best interest to be testing more. Yeah. Um. Last thing—uh— is kind of just like diversifying spend and risk. So the way that I see an ad account is just like your mini hedge fund. It's like, okay, I'm spending 100k or 200k a month in capital, and your job as a media buyer, as an offer owner, is just to maximize your return across the entire portfolio while also managing your downside risk. So what we've found is that if we run just one conversion mechanism, like just a call funnel— especially at high levels of spend— you kind of have these almost like imaginary ceilings where it's like, when you try to push past them, you have diminishing returns and things start to break. You really feel like that volatility. So, like the day is when we're only spending like $15,000 on a call funnel, or what you'll have days where like the leave quality just completely goes to shit and the cost per call completely goes to shit. Basically, we have ways of mitigating this, but one of these is just horizontally scaling campaigns. So we never have like one campaign that's just getting all the spend— we have some that are getting proportionally more spend than others. Like I said, Like we want to have stability in our ad account rather than just like juicing the winners for all that they got. And then also just running multiple funnels. Also like other tools like LeadFi, stuff like that we use. So the whole thought process around having multiple funnels is just like having two kidneys. It's like if one of them fails, you still have another one. So, like I said, mitigates risk. And we found that most funnels have diminishing returns at X ceiling. Like, every time we've tried to go past that, it gets less effective. 13 or 14k a day on the call funnel usually doesn't go well, so we're basically figuring out where to put the rest of that capital to get a better return, without just trying to put push past the ceiling. So yeah, like, overall, the biggest levers on scaling to a million a month is the offer niche, vertical positioning. That stuff just about like what you're selling— the market you're selling in, etc. The funnels you use, financially modeling, and then just strict KPI upkeep. So like, it's one thing to like just see your numbers, but it's another thing to actually maintain your numbers over time. Messaging, creative velocity, sales team, staying level-headed. And basically, just like when things are going good, don't get too high. When things are going bad, don't get too low. And obviously like getting help, like using your resources in this group, et cetera. And then just everyone being mentally bought in. There was another side, but either way, I just want to thank Jeremy for the way he runs the group and how unselfish he is with it and for always pushing us. Hell yeah. Valeu.
